title: Chapter 16: The Fox's Cigarette and Ash
paragraphs: Rumors spread along the Southern Tract faster than a plague, soaking into the minds of travelers along with the caustic dust of a crumbling world. A goblin saved an elven caravan. A goblin. Saved. An elven. Caravan. Every word in that sentence shattered the fundamental laws of existence, challenging an age-old order of things that had seemed unshakable even in the face of rotting skies.

Some called it the invention of a merchant maddened by fear. Others, a cunning illusion of demons. But a red-haired guard named Taeron, whose side was tightly bound with blood-soaked bandages, told the story to everyone he met on the path to the ancient rune gates of Agdash. In his eyes, there was not a trace of feverish delirium. There stood a quiet, almost painful realization that for his entire long life, he had despised the wrong creatures.

Yashil knew nothing of this. Mundane fame held no weight for him. He returned to his damp burrow and continued to live in the rhythm of a fading world: sitting for long periods on the edge of the ravine, listening to the rustle of dead feather grass, helping old Murka with her young, and waiting for nightly meetings with Kirah. The werewolf Janavar, to his surprise, also did not dissolve back into the wastes.
The massive wolf had made a lair for himself in the ruins of an old mill, whose millstones had long since grown over with toxic blue moss, and appeared exactly when the silence became too heavy.

"A true lone wolf doesn't tie himself to a place, Greenie," Janavar philosophized one evening, stretching out on the broken roof of the mill and lazily picking at a bone stuck in his teeth with a claw. "I've simply... strategically secured the high ground. For perimeter control. Tactics and dominance, brother. Auf."
"You just don't want to fall asleep alone in the dark," Yashil replied calmly, not taking his eyes off the horizon.
"Low blow," the werewolf winced, closing his eyes. "You hit harder with words than those bandits do with steel."
She appeared in the third week after the blood had dried on the Southern Tract.

Yashil was sitting in his usual spot when the wind suddenly shifted. Instead of the familiar scent of ozone, rot, and burnt copper, the air brought something alien: the sweetish aroma of old, faded perfume, the tart smell of smoldering tobacco and pine.
The goblin slowly turned around.

On a fallen, withered tree trunk behind him sat a being made of contradictions. A graceful humanoid body was encased in a worn leather corset and torn stockings, over which hung a heavy cloak of fine elven craftsmanship. Hands were hidden in long fingerless gloves, quietly jingling with old bracelets.

But the head... It was a fox's muzzle. Elongated, red, with sharp, sensitively twitching ears and clever, amber eyes. Those eyes looked at the world with such deep, ancient, and absolutely sincere weariness that a single glance made one want to apologize for the very fact of one's existence.

The fox held a long bone mouthpiece in her thin, clawed fingers, from which wound a thin stream of acrid, greenish smoke. Dark lips were touched with cherry paint, and on her eyelids lay thick shadows, completely out of place in this dying world.
She took a deep drag, released an ideal smoke ring into the ashen sky, and spoke in a voice like silk soaked in poison and old wine:
"So, you are that same idiot."
Yashil blinked, not moving from his spot.

"The very same goblin who threw himself at a dozen cutthroats with a burning stick to save elves who for millennia considered his race a mistake of nature," she continued, gracefully crossing her legs. "It's impressive. Infinitely stupid, but impressive. I am Renée."

She held out a gloved hand to him. The gesture was slow, as if giving him time to change his mind. Yashil, looking at the elegant claws, calmly shook her hand. Renée's fingers were unexpectedly strong and cold.
"Why have you come to Rotten Hollow?" Yashil asked quietly.
Renée took another drag, squinting her amber eyes.

"Boredom, my dear green friend. A monstrous, all-consuming boredom from which the soul rots," she flicked ash onto the dead earth. "I've traveled half of this doomed continent. Robbed the treasuries of three lords, slept in silks, broke a dozen hearts in high towers, and I still have nothing to occupy myself. And then—a goblin who intends to save a fading world with kindness. This," she pointed the mouthpiece directly at him, "is the most absurd and intriguing thing I've encountered in the last half-century."
"I'm not trying to save the world, Renée," Yashil shook his head. "That's impossible."

"Oh, I know. But you are trying to save an idea. Which is even more meaningless, and therefore—even more beautiful," the fox smiled faintly, only with the corners of her lips. In that smile there was no warmth, only the bitter irony of a being who had outlived her own hopes. "I think I'll stay for a while. Watch from the front row as this universe breaks your illusions."

Kirah came at night, stepping with heavy paws over the withered grass with a grace not natural for such a massive creature. Seeing Renée sitting by a tiny campfire, the demon froze. Two eyes burning with red fire in a black skull widened. Killer instincts kicked in instantly: claws slid from his fingers with a rasp, and his tail tensed.
"This... what is this?" Kirah rasped, warily eyeing the fox.
"This is Renée," Yashil answered calmly, tossing a dry branch into the fire. "She's... a friend. I think."

"I am an observer," Renée corrected lazily, not looking away from her eternal cigarette. The firelight reflected in her eyes like golden sparks. "The word 'friend' implies obligations. And obligations are tiring and ruin the complexion."
Kirah stood frozen, towering over her like a black mountain. He was created to tear worlds apart, but before the naked, bored cynicism of this fox, he found himself completely defenseless.
Renée measured the giant with an evaluating gaze.

"A demon of the legion who is afraid to cause pain, down to his trembling knees," she stated, releasing a plume of smoke. "And a fox who desperately pretends not to care about anything. We both lie to ourselves, horned one. The only difference is that I have enough style to do it gracefully."
Kirah opened his tooth-filled maw, trying to find a worthy response, but the words stuck in his throat.

The silence was broken by the crunch of branches. From the pitch darkness, Janavar stepped into the firelight, dragging the heavy carcass of a killed cave boar. The werewolf dropped the prey by the fire, wiped his bloody maw with the back of a paw, and scanned the assembled company. A small green-skinned goblin-pacifist, a gargantuan rogue demon suffering from soul-aches, and a fox in a corset, smoking through a mouthpiece.
Janavar grinned in a wide, eerie smirk and sat on his haunches.

"Now this is what I'm talking about," the wolf drawled in a velvety bass. "A flawlessly ridiculous pack. You know what the wise men said? It doesn't matter who stands against you if those sitting next to you are the ones you can share a piece of raw meat with. Auf, ladies and gentlemen of the jury. I definitely like it here."
Thus, around a single dim campfire, a company gathered that no chronicler of Agdash would dare describe in their works.
The Goblin, the Demon, the Werewolf, and the Fox.

Four absolute outcasts. Each of them had been stricken, rejected, and cursed by the laws of this world. But here, listening to the crackle of burning branches and the howl of the icy wasteland wind, they paradoxically complemented each other.

Yashil looked at the sparks flying into the indifferent, rotting black sky. For the first time in his short life, he realized a simple but heavy truth. Perhaps, to oppose the end of the world, one doesn't need an army of steel-clad righteous men. Perhaps, four broken beings are enough—beings who had the courage to stay together when everyone else chose to scatter into the darkness.
